title: Debugging & Profiling Tools
description: Perfetto, LeakCanary, Android Studio Profiler, StrictMode, Layout Inspector, and systematic debugging

references.title: Android Developers — Inspect traces with Perfetto

lesson:
Debugging & Profiling Tool Overview

Knowing which tool to reach for is half the battle. Each tool targets a specific class of problem:

ToolWhat It FindsWhen to UseOverhead
Perfetto / SystraceJank, slow frames, binder contention, thread schedulingApp feels slow, frames drop, ANR suspectedLow (kernel-level tracing)
LeakCanaryMemory leaks (retained objects after GC)Memory grows over time, OOM crashesDebug builds only
Android Studio ProfilerCPU hot spots, memory allocations, energy drainGeneral profiling, allocation tracking, battery analysisMedium
StrictModeDisk/network on main thread, leaked closablesDevelopment-time policy enforcementLow (detection only)
Layout InspectorCompose recomposition counts, view hierarchy depthUI not updating, excessive recompositionLow (snapshot)
MAT (Memory Analyzer)Heap object graph, dominator trees, retained sizeDeep memory investigation, hprof analysisOffline (post-capture)

Perfetto / Systrace: System-Wide Tracing

Perfetto is the modern replacement for Systrace. It captures kernel-level scheduling, Choreographer frame timing, binder transactions, and custom app trace sections — all in one timeline.

Capturing a Trace via ADB

# Record a 10-second trace with key categories adb shell perfetto \ -c - --txt \ -o /data/misc/perfetto-traces/trace.perfetto-trace \ <# Pull and open in https://ui.perfetto.dev/ adb pull /data/misc/perfetto-traces/trace.perfetto-trace .

What to Look For in a Perfetto Trace

Key Trace Markers for Frame Performance:
Choreographer#doFrame — one slice per frame; should be < 16.6 ms (60 fps) or < 8.3 ms (120 fps)
measure / layout / draw — the three View phases inside each frame; look for outliers
RenderThread — GPU command submission; long slices = overdraw or complex shaders
Binder transactions — synchronous IPC on the main thread blocks frames; look for binder:reply slices
SurfaceFlinger — compositor; if it drops your buffer, the issue is on the system side
Perfetto Timeline (one janky frame): Main Thread: ──[Choreographer#doFrame 28ms]────────────────── ├─[measure 2ms] ├─[layout 1ms] ├─[draw 4ms] └─[binder:reply 18ms] ← BLOCKING IPC! RenderThread: ─────────────────────[syncAndDraw 3ms]────────── SurfaceFlinger:──────────────────────────[missed vsync!]────── Diagnosis: Binder call on main thread during frame = jank. Fix: Move the binder call off main thread.

Custom Trace Sections

Add your own markers to Perfetto traces to measure specific code paths:

// Java/Kotlin — manual begin/end import android.os.Trace fun loadDashboard() { Trace.beginSection("DashboardLoad") try { val data = repository.fetchData() renderDashboard(data) } finally { Trace.endSection() } } // Kotlin inline helper — no try/finally boilerplate inline fun <T> trace(sectionName: String, block: () -> T): T { Trace.beginSection(sectionName) return try { block() } finally { Trace.endSection() } } // Usage — shows up as named slices in Perfetto trace("ParseJSON") { gson.fromJson(json, MyData::class.java) } trace("ImageDecode") { BitmapFactory.decodeStream(stream) }
Trace section names are truncated to 127 characters. Keep them short but descriptive. Also, Trace.beginSection / endSection must be called on the same thread — for async work use Trace.beginAsyncSection / endAsyncSection with a matching cookie integer.

LeakCanary: Automatic Leak Detection

LeakCanary detects memory leaks at runtime with zero configuration. Here is exactly how it works under the hood:

LeakCanary Detection Flow: 1. Activity/Fragment destroyed │ ▼ 2. LeakCanary wraps it in a WeakReference with a unique key │ ▼ 3. Waits 5 seconds, then triggers GC │ ▼ 4. Checks: was the WeakReference cleared? │ ┌───┴───┐ │ YES │ NO → Object was NOT collected → LEAK! │ │ │ │ OK ✓ │ ▼ │ │ 5. Dumps heap (hprof file) │ │ │ │ │ ▼ │ │ 6. Analyzes hprof to find the │ │ SHORTEST reference chain from │ │ GC root → leaked object │ │ │ │ │ ▼ │ │ 7. Shows notification with the └───────┘ leak trace (like a stack trace but for object references)

Zero-Config Setup

// build.gradle.kts — that's it, nothing else needed dependencies { debugImplementation("com.squareup.leakcanary:leakcanary-android:2.14") } // LeakCanary auto-installs via a ContentProvider. // No Application subclass code needed. // It watches Activities, Fragments, Views, ViewModels, // and Services automatically.

Reading a Leak Trace

// Example LeakCanary output: ┬─── │ GC Root: Global variable in system class │ ├─ com.example.app.AudioManager instance │ Leaking: NO (singleton) │ ↓ AudioManager.listener │ ~~~~~~~~ ├─ com.example.app.MusicFragment$1 instance ← anonymous inner class │ Leaking: UNKNOWN │ ↓ MusicFragment$1.this$0 │ ~~~~~~ ╰─ com.example.app.MusicFragment instance Leaking: YES (Fragment#mView is null) // Translation: AudioManager holds a reference to an // anonymous listener, which holds an implicit reference // to MusicFragment. The fragment is destroyed but can't // be garbage collected. // Fix: Unregister the listener in onDestroyView().

The 5 Most Common Android Memory Leaks

#PatternWhy It LeaksFix
1Unregistered listeners/callbacksSingleton holds reference to Activity/Fragment via callbackUnregister in onStop() or onDestroyView()
2Anonymous inner classesInner class holds implicit this$0 reference to enclosing ActivityUse static class + WeakReference, or lambda without capturing this
3Static Context referencecompanion object or static field holds Activity context foreverUse applicationContext or clear the reference
4Fragment View binding not nulledFragment outlives its view; binding holds view referencesSet _binding = null in onDestroyView()
5GlobalScope coroutinesCoroutine captures Activity/ViewModel reference, never cancelledUse viewModelScope or lifecycleScope
// LEAK #1: Unregistered listener class PlayerActivity : AppCompatActivity() { override fun onCreate(savedInstanceState: Bundle?) { super.onCreate(savedInstanceState) AudioManager.addPlaybackListener(this) // registers } // BUG: Never calls AudioManager.removePlaybackListener(this) // Fix: override onDestroy() and unregister } // LEAK #2: Anonymous inner class capturing Activity class ChatActivity : AppCompatActivity() { fun sendMessage() { api.send(message, object : Callback { override fun onSuccess() { // 'this@ChatActivity' is captured implicitly! showToast("Sent") } }) } } // LEAK #4: Fragment binding not nulled class ListFragment : Fragment() { private var _binding: FragmentListBinding? = null private val binding get() = _binding!! override fun onDestroyView() { super.onDestroyView() _binding = null // REQUIRED — without this, leaked! } }

Android Studio Profiler

The integrated profiler has three panels: CPU, Memory, and Energy. Each reveals different classes of problems.

CPU Profiler

Two recording modes:
Sample-based — captures call stacks at intervals (low overhead, good for production-like profiling)
Instrumented — wraps every method entry/exit (high overhead, exact timing, misses nothing)

Flame Chart — x-axis is time, y-axis is call stack depth. Wide bars = slow methods. Look for flat plateaus (long-running methods) and deep stacks (excessive recursion/nesting).
Reading a Flame Chart: ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ main() │ ← widest = total time ├──────────────────┬──────────────────────┤ │ loadData() │ renderUI() │ ├────────┬─────────┤ │ │ parse()│ decode() │ │ ├────────┘ │ │ │ │ │ Wide + flat = hot spot. Narrow + deep = okay. Key insight: Look for YOUR code that's wide. Library code at the bottom is expected.

Memory Profiler

Allocation tracking shows every object allocated and where. The memory graph tells a story:

Steady line — healthy, stable memory
Sawtooth pattern (up-down-up-down) — GC pressure! Objects allocated and immediately discarded. Common cause: allocating in onDraw() or onBindViewHolder()
Staircase pattern (up-flat-up-flat) — memory leak! Objects accumulate and are never freed
Sudden spike — large allocation (bitmap, buffer). May cause OOM on low-memory devices.

Energy Profiler

Shows what is draining the battery:

Drain SourceWhat to Look ForFix
Radio wakeFrequent short network requests waking the cellular radioBatch requests, use WorkManager for deferrable work
GPS / LocationContinuous high-accuracy location updatesUse PRIORITY_BALANCED_POWER_ACCURACY, increase interval
WakelocksCPU or screen kept on longer than neededRelease wakelocks promptly, use timeout parameter

StrictMode: Catch Problems During Development

StrictMode detects things you accidentally do on the wrong thread, plus VM-level resource leaks. Enable it early in your Application class:

class MyApplication : Application() { override fun onCreate() { super.onCreate() if (BuildConfig.DEBUG) { // ThreadPolicy: what you must NOT do on the main thread StrictMode.setThreadPolicy( StrictMode.ThreadPolicy.Builder() .detectDiskReads() // SharedPreferences on main! .detectDiskWrites() // File I/O on main! .detectNetwork() // Network on main! (also throws NetworkOnMainThreadException) .penaltyLog() // Log to Logcat .penaltyDeath() // Crash the app (aggressive but effective) .build() ) // VmPolicy: detect resource leaks across any thread StrictMode.setVmPolicy( StrictMode.VmPolicy.Builder() .detectLeakedClosableObjects() // Cursor, InputStream not closed .detectLeakedSqlLiteObjects() // SQLite cursors not closed .detectActivityLeaks() // Activity instance leaked .penaltyLog() .build() ) } } }
StrictMode should only be enabled in debug builds. In release builds, the overhead and crashes are unacceptable. Guard with BuildConfig.DEBUG. Also note: penaltyDeath() crashes the app on the first violation — great for CI, but annoying during casual development. Use penaltyLog() alone during exploration.

Compose Recomposition Tracking

In Jetpack Compose, performance problems usually mean too many recompositions or unstable parameters. The Layout Inspector visualizes both:

Layout Inspector → Recomposition Counts:
Recomposition count — how many times a composable re-executed. High count on a static composable = bug
Skip count — how many times Compose SKIPPED recomposition because inputs didn't change. High skip count = good (Compose is smart)
Stability — whether the compiler marked parameters as stable. Unstable params = recompose EVERY time parent recomposes
Layout Inspector Recomposition View: Composable Recompositions Skipped ────────────────── ────────────── ─────── ChatScreen 1 0 MessageList 12 0 ← recomposing a LOT MessageItem (0) 1 11 ← good: skips often MessageItem (1) 12 0 ← BAD: never skips! MessageItem (2) 1 11 Why does MessageItem(1) never skip? → Its parameter is an UNSTABLE class. → Compose can't prove equality, so it recomposes every time. Fix: Mark the data class @Immutable or @Stable, or ensure all its fields are primitive/immutable types.
// UNSTABLE — List is a mutable interface, Compose can't trust it data class Message( val id: String, val text: String, val reactions: List<Reaction> // List = unstable! ) // STABLE — use kotlinx.collections.immutable import kotlinx.collections.immutable.ImmutableList @Immutable data class Message( val id: String, val text: String, val reactions: ImmutableList<Reaction> // ImmutableList = stable ✓ )

Debugging Decision Tree

When something is wrong, start here:

What's the symptom? │ ├─ "App is janky / frames drop" │ └─→ Perfetto trace │ Look at: Choreographer#doFrame duration, │ binder calls on main thread, long measure/layout/draw │ ├─ "Memory keeps growing" / OOM crash │ └─→ LeakCanary (auto-detection) │ └─→ Android Studio Memory Profiler (allocation tracking) │ └─→ MAT (deep hprof analysis) │ Pattern: Staircase graph = leak. Sawtooth = GC pressure. │ ├─ "Battery drains fast" │ └─→ Android Studio Energy Profiler │ Look at: radio wakes, GPS usage, held wakelocks │ ├─ "UI not updating" / "wrong state" │ └─→ Layout Inspector │ Check: recomposition counts, stability annotations │ ├─ "App crashes with ANR" │ └─→ StrictMode (prevention) + Perfetto (diagnosis) │ Look at: main thread blocked > 5 seconds │ Common: DB query, network, file I/O on main thread │ └─ "Slow startup" └─→ Perfetto (app startup metric) + Android Studio CPU Profiler (flame chart) Look at: Application.onCreate(), ContentProviders, Dagger/Hilt injection time

Debugging Methodology — Step-by-Step Flows

Knowing the tools is necessary but not sufficient. Debugging is a systematic process — reproduce, capture, analyze, fix, verify. These flows show exactly how to diagnose five common production problems from symptom to resolution.

Flow 1: "App drops frames during AI response streaming"

Step-by-Step: Diagnosing Streaming Jank 1. REPRODUCE └─ Stream a long AI response → watch for visible jank (stuttering scroll, frozen UI, dropped animations) 2. CAPTURE: Perfetto trace └─ adb shell perfetto -t 10s sched freq idle am wm gfx view (Records 10 seconds of scheduling, graphics, view system) 3. OPEN in ui.perfetto.dev └─ Find your app's main thread in the process list 4. FIND Choreographer#doFrame slices └─ Each slice = one frame. Sort by duration. Look for slices > 16.6ms (60fps) or > 8.3ms (120fps) 5. IS doFrame > 16ms? → YES → Which phase is slow? │ ├─ traversal (measure/layout) > 10ms │ └─ View hierarchy too complex, OR │ Text composable measuring a huge string each frame │ Fix: Use LazyColumn, chunk the text, avoid │ measuring the entire response on every frame │ ├─ draw > 5ms │ └─ Overdraw, complex shapes, or large bitmap │ Fix: Reduce overdraw, simplify drawing, │ use hardware layers for static content │ └─ input > 2ms └─ Touch handler doing too much work Fix: Defer processing, debounce input 6. CHECK RenderThread └─ Is GPU work backing up? dequeueBuffer slow = GPU backpressure (GPU can't keep up with frame production) 7. CHECK for binder transactions in frame └─ Synchronous IPC blocking the frame? binder:reply slices inside doFrame = problem 8. FIX based on finding: └─ Chunk the text (don't set entire response each frame) └─ Use LazyColumn for long responses └─ Move text parsing/formatting off main thread └─ Use Modifier.drawWithCache for expensive drawing

Flow 2: "App uses too much battery in the background"

Step-by-Step: Diagnosing Battery Drain 1. REPRODUCE └─ Install app, use it normally, leave in background overnight (Real-world usage pattern, not synthetic test) 2. CAPTURE battery stats └─ adb shell dumpsys batterystats --reset (clear old data) └─ ... wait several hours ... └─ adb bugreport (capture everything) 3. OPEN in Battery Historian (https://bathist.ef.lc/) └─ Upload the bugreport zip file └─ Find your app in the app list 4. LOOK FOR these battery killers: │ ├─ WAKELOCKS (your app holding CPU awake) │ └─ Symptom: wakelock bars in the timeline │ └─ Check: wakelock name, duration, count │ Fix: Add timeout to acquire() │ PowerManager.WakeLock.acquire(10 * 60 * 1000L) │ ├─ NETWORK ACTIVITY (radio wake) │ └─ Symptom: frequent short radio-on periods │ └─ Each radio wake costs ~20 seconds of power │ Fix: Batch requests, switch polling → FCM push │ ├─ ALARMS (AlarmManager) │ └─ Symptom: frequent alarm triggers in background │ Fix: Use inexact alarms, respect Doze mode, │ migrate to WorkManager for deferrable work │ └─ GPS / LOCATION └─ Symptom: GPS-on bars in timeline └─ Check: accuracy level and update interval Fix: Use PRIORITY_BALANCED_POWER_ACCURACY instead of HIGH_ACCURACY when approximate location is sufficient 5. COMMON CULPRITS: └─ Wakelock held without timeout → add timeout └─ Polling instead of push → switch to FCM └─ Location at HIGH_ACCURACY → BALANCED works fine └─ Network calls not batched → use WorkManager periodic 6. VERIFY: Compare before/after batterystats └─ Run same test, compare Battery Historian output

Flow 3: "Memory keeps growing, app eventually OOM kills"

Step-by-Step: Diagnosing Memory Growth → OOM 1. REPRODUCE └─ Use the app: navigate between screens, rotate device, open/close features multiple times └─ Watch memory trend over several minutes 2. WATCH in Android Studio Memory Profiler: │ ├─ SAWTOOTH pattern (up-down-up-down rapidly) │ └─ = GC pressure (too many short-lived allocations) │ └─ Find the hot allocation path: │ Allocation Profiler → sort by count → find │ onBindViewHolder/onDraw creating objects │ Fix: Pre-allocate objects, hoist out of hot loops │ ├─ STAIRCASE pattern (up-flat-up-flat, never drops) │ └─ = Memory leak (objects retained that shouldn't be) │ └─ Use LeakCanary (see step 3) │ └─ SPIKE (sudden large allocation) └─ = Large single allocation (bitmap, JSON parse) Fix: Sample/downsample bitmaps, stream JSON parsing, use BitmapFactory.Options.inSampleSize 3. IF STAIRCASE (leak suspected): └─ Trigger LeakCanary by navigating away from screens └─ Wait for notification → read the reference chain └─ The chain shows: GC root → ... → leaked object └─ Find where YOUR code appears → that's the fix point 4. COMMON LEAKS: └─ Static Context reference (companion object holds Activity) └─ Unregistered listener (singleton holds callback → Fragment) └─ Fragment View binding not nulled in onDestroyView() └─ CoroutineScope not cancelled (GlobalScope capturing Activity) 5. IF SAWTOOTH (GC pressure): └─ Allocation Profiler → record during scrolling └─ Sort by allocation count (not size) └─ Look for: Paint, Rect, String in onDraw/onBind Fix: Move allocations to init{} or constructor, use object pools, avoid string concatenation in loops

Flow 4: "ANR reported in production — users see 'App Not Responding'"

Step-by-Step: Diagnosing ANR 1. GET THE ANR TRACE └─ Play Console → Android Vitals → ANRs OR from device: /data/anr/traces.txt └─ The trace shows every thread's stack at the moment the main thread was blocked for 5+ seconds 2. LOOK AT THE MAIN THREAD STACK └─ What was the main thread doing when it froze? └─ Read from bottom (entry point) to top (blocked call) └─ The top frame is WHERE it's stuck 3. COMMON CAUSES: │ ├─ Synchronous database query on main thread │ └─ Stack shows: SQLiteDatabase.query() or Room DAO │ Fix: Move to withContext(Dispatchers.IO) │ Room already supports suspend functions │ ├─ Synchronous network call │ └─ Stack shows: OkHttp, HttpURLConnection, Socket.read() │ Fix: Move to coroutine with Dispatchers.IO │ (Android 11+ throws NetworkOnMainThreadException │ but older versions just ANR) │ ├─ Lock contention │ └─ Stack shows: Object.wait(), ReentrantLock.lock() │ └─ Main thread waiting for a lock held by │ a background thread doing slow work │ Fix: Use Mutex (coroutines), restructure to avoid │ shared mutable state, use lock-free data structures │ └─ Binder transaction timeout └─ Stack shows: BinderProxy.transact() └─ Calling a slow system service synchronously └─ ContentResolver, PackageManager, etc. Fix: Move system service calls off main thread 4. PREVENTION: StrictMode catches these in development └─ StrictMode.ThreadPolicy.Builder() .detectDiskReads() .detectDiskWrites() .detectNetwork() .penaltyDeath() ← crash in debug, not ANR in prod .build()
ANR timeout is 5 seconds for foreground activities and 10 seconds for broadcast receivers. But the user perceives jank much sooner — any main thread block over 100ms is noticeable. StrictMode with penaltyDeath() catches these problems before they become production ANRs.

Flow 5: "Compose recomposition causing jank on a specific screen"

Step-by-Step: Diagnosing Compose Recomposition Jank 1. OPEN Layout Inspector └─ Enable "Show Recomposition Counts" └─ Connect to running app 2. INTERACT with the screen └─ Perform the action that causes jank └─ Watch which composables light up with high counts 3. IDENTIFY problematic composables: └─ High recomposition count + LOW skip count = PROBLEM (Compose recomposes every time, never skips) └─ High recomposition count + HIGH skip count = OK (Compose is efficiently skipping when inputs unchanged) Example: Composable Recompositions Skipped ChatScreen 50 0 ← recomposes, expected MessageList 50 0 ← recomposes, expected MessageItem(0) 50 0 ← BAD! Never skips! MessageItem(1) 1 49 ← Good, skips often MessageItem(2) 1 49 ← Good, skips often 4. CHECK: Why doesn't it skip? │ ├─ Receiving a List (unstable)? │ └─ List is a mutable interface → Compose can't trust it │ Fix: Use ImmutableList from kotlinx.collections.immutable │ ├─ Receiving a lambda (new instance each recomposition)? │ └─ { viewModel.doThing(item.id) } creates new lambda object │ Fix: Extract to remember { { viewModel.doThing(item.id) } } │ or use method reference │ ├─ Receiving a data class from external module? │ └─ Compose compiler can't verify stability across modules │ Fix: Add @Immutable or @Stable annotation │ └─ Missing key in LazyColumn? └─ Items shift positions → wrong items recompose Fix: LazyColumn { items(list, key = { it.id }) { ... } } 5. FIX and verify: └─ Apply fix → recomposition count should drop └─ Skip count should increase └─ Frame timing (Perfetto) should improve └─ Visual jank should disappear
Quick stability check without Layout Inspector: Add the Compose compiler report to your build:
kotlinOptions { freeCompilerArgs += listOf("-P", "plugin:androidx.compose.compiler.plugins.kotlin:reportsDestination=" + buildDir + "/compose_reports") }
This generates a report showing which classes are stable/unstable and which composables are skippable/restartable.

questions.title: Choosing the Right Tool
questions.description: Your app's memory usage graph shows a staircase pattern — memory rises in steps and never drops back down. Which tool should you reach for first?
options: Perfetto — to look at frame timing
LeakCanary — to detect which objects are being retained
Energy Profiler — to check for wakelock issues
StrictMode — to detect disk reads on main thread
questions.explanation: A staircase memory pattern (up-flat-up-flat) is the classic signature of a memory leak — objects are being allocated and never freed. LeakCanary detects exactly this: it watches destroyed Activities/Fragments, forces GC, and if the object survives, dumps the heap and shows the reference chain keeping it alive. The Memory Profiler confirms the pattern, but LeakCanary pinpoints the cause.
hints: A staircase pattern means objects accumulate and are never collected
Which tool specializes in finding retained objects after GC?

questions.title: Interpreting a Perfetto Trace
questions.description: You see this in a Perfetto trace. What is the problem and how would you fix it?
options: The measure/layout/draw phases are too slow — simplify the view hierarchy
RenderThread is blocked — GPU is overloaded with overdraw
A synchronous binder (IPC) call on the main thread is blocking for 35ms, causing the frame to exceed the 16.6ms budget
SurfaceFlinger is dropping buffers — this is a system-level issue
questions.explanation: The measure (1ms), layout (1ms), and draw (2ms) phases are fast. The problem is the binder:reply slice taking 35ms — this is a synchronous IPC call happening on the main thread during frame rendering. The total frame time is 42ms, well over the 16.6ms budget for 60fps. Fix: move the binder call (likely a system service query like PackageManager or ContentResolver) off the main thread using withContext(Dispatchers.IO).
hints: Look at the time breakdown — which slice dominates the 42ms?
Binder calls are inter-process communication — they block until the other process responds

questions.title: LeakCanary Leak Trace
questions.description:
You see this LeakCanary trace. What is the leak and how do you fix it?
┬───
│ GC Root: Global variable in system class
│
├─ com.example.app.EventBus instance
│    Leaking: NO (singleton)
│    ↓ EventBus.listeners
├─ java.util.ArrayList
│    ↓ ArrayList[2]
├─ com.example.app.ProfileFragment instance
│    Leaking: YES (Fragment#mView is null)
╰───
options: EventBus is leaking because it's a singleton — replace with Hilt injection
ArrayList is the wrong collection type — replace with LinkedList
ProfileFragment registered as a listener on EventBus but never unregistered, so the singleton holds a reference to the destroyed fragment
The fragment's view binding needs to be nulled in onDestroyView()
questions.explanation: The leak trace shows: EventBus (singleton, NOT leaking) holds an ArrayList of listeners, which contains ProfileFragment (IS leaking — its view is null, meaning it's been destroyed). The fragment registered itself as a listener but never called EventBus.unregister(this) in onDestroyView() or onDestroy(). This is leak pattern #1: unregistered listeners. Fix: override fun onDestroyView() { EventBus.unregister(this); super.onDestroyView() }.
hints: Follow the reference chain from GC root to the leaked object
What is keeping the fragment alive after it was destroyed?

questions.title: Memory Profiler Sawtooth Pattern
questions.description: The Memory Profiler shows a rapid sawtooth pattern — memory repeatedly climbs and then sharply drops. What does this indicate and what's the likely cause?
options: Memory leak — objects are never freed
GC pressure — objects are rapidly allocated and discarded, triggering frequent garbage collection. Likely cause: allocating objects in onDraw(), onBindViewHolder(), or a tight loop
Normal healthy behavior — the GC is working as expected
The heap is too small — increase it with android:largeHeap="true"
questions.explanation: A sawtooth pattern means the app allocates objects rapidly, filling the heap, then GC runs and reclaims them, the heap drops, and the cycle repeats. This causes GC pressure — each GC pause (even a concurrent one) steals CPU time and can cause micro-jank. Common causes: allocating Paint, Rect, or String objects inside onDraw(), creating new objects in onBindViewHolder() for every scroll event, or string concatenation in loops. Fix: pre-allocate reusable objects and hoist allocations out of hot paths.
hints: Sawtooth = up-down-up-down in rapid succession
What happens when you allocate objects that are immediately discardable?

questions.title: StrictMode Violations
questions.description: You add StrictMode to your app and immediately see this in Logcat. What is happening and how should you fix it?
questions.code:
D/StrictMode: StrictMode policy violation:
  android.os.StrictMode$StrictModeDiskReadViolation
    at android.app.SharedPreferencesImpl.getAll(SharedPreferencesImpl.java:312)
    at com.example.app.SettingsRepository.getUserPrefs(SettingsRepository.kt:24)
    at com.example.app.MainActivity.onCreate(MainActivity.kt:38)
options: SharedPreferences is corrupted and needs to be cleared
SharedPreferences is reading from disk on the main thread in onCreate() — move the read to a background thread or migrate to DataStore
StrictMode is buggy and produces false positives for SharedPreferences
This only happens on first launch and can be safely ignored
questions.explanation: StrictMode detected a disk read on the main thread. SharedPreferences.getAll() reads the XML file from disk synchronously. While it's cached after the first read, that first read can take 10-100ms on older devices or with large preference files — blocking the main thread during onCreate() and delaying the first frame. Fix options: (1) use apply() instead of commit() for writes, (2) move reads to Dispatchers.IO with coroutines, or (3) migrate to Jetpack DataStore which is async by default.
hints: The violation type tells you exactly what happened: DiskReadViolation
The stack trace shows it's called from MainActivity.onCreate — the main thread

questions.title: Compose Recomposition Debugging
questions.description: In the Layout Inspector, you see that UserCard has 47 recompositions and 0 skips, while its parent UserList has 47 recompositions. All other siblings have 1 recomposition and 46 skips. What is the most likely cause?
options: UserCard has a bug in its remember{} calls
UserCard receives an unstable parameter — Compose cannot prove equality, so it recomposes every time its parent recomposes
UserList is using LazyColumn incorrectly
There is a side effect inside UserCard triggering infinite recomposition
questions.explanation: When a composable has a high recomposition count and zero skips, while its siblings skip frequently, the problem is almost always an unstable parameter. Compose skips recomposition when all parameters are equal to the previous call. But if a parameter type is unstable (e.g., contains List, Map, or a class from an external module), Compose cannot prove equality and must recompose every time. Fix: mark the data class @Immutable or @Stable, or use kotlinx.collections.immutable types for collection fields.
hints: 0 skips means Compose NEVER decided it could skip — why?
Compare with siblings: they skip 46/47 times. What's different about UserCard's inputs?

questions.title: Fix the Memory Leak
questions.description: This Fragment leaks its view. Fix the code so the view reference is properly cleaned up.

checks.message: Null out the binding reference in onDestroyView()
Unregister the location listener in onDestroyView()

questions.explanation: Two leaks fixed: (1) _binding = null releases the view references — without this, the Fragment (which outlives its view during back-stack transactions) holds dead view references. (2) removeLocationListener unregisters the callback — without this, the LocationManager singleton holds a reference to the Fragment forever. Bonus: setting recyclerView.adapter = null prevents the RecyclerView from holding adapter/ViewHolder references after the view is destroyed.
hints: Two things need cleanup: the view binding AND the listener registration
Fragments can outlive their views — onDestroyView() is called but onDestroy() is not

questions.title: CPU Profiler — Sample vs Instrumented
questions.description: You want to measure the exact execution time of each method in your JSON parsing pipeline. Which CPU profiling mode should you use?
options: Sample-based — it has lower overhead and is more accurate for timing
Instrumented — it wraps every method entry/exit so you get exact call counts and durations, at the cost of higher overhead
Either mode works identically for this use case
Neither — use Perfetto instead
questions.explanation: Instrumented mode wraps every method call with timing instrumentation, giving you exact entry/exit timestamps and call counts. This is ideal when you need precise timing of specific methods (like a parsing pipeline). The tradeoff is higher overhead — the app runs slower during profiling, which can distort wall-clock measurements. Sample-based mode periodically snapshots the call stack (e.g., every 1ms), so it has low overhead but can miss short methods. Use sample-based for "where is time going overall?" and instrumented for "how long does this specific method take?"
hints: You need EXACT timing for each method — which mode measures every entry and exit?
Consider the trade-off: precision vs overhead

questions.title: Identifying the Leak Pattern
questions.description: What memory leak pattern does this code exhibit, and what will LeakCanary report?
options: No leak — companion object references are automatically cleared

Leak pattern #3: Static Context reference. The companion object (static field) holds the Activity after it is destroyed. LeakCanary will show a chain from GC root (class static field) → lastActivity → VideoPlayerActivity
Leak pattern #2: Anonymous inner class holding implicit reference
Leak pattern #5: GlobalScope coroutine capturing the Activity
questions.explanation: This is leak pattern #3: static Context reference. The companion object property lastActivity is a static field at the JVM level. It holds a strong reference to the Activity instance. When the Activity is destroyed (e.g., on rotation), the static field still points to the old instance, preventing garbage collection. Every rotation creates a new Activity while leaking the old one. Fix: clear the reference in onDestroy() or, better yet, avoid storing Activity references in static fields entirely — use a ViewModel or application-scoped service instead.
hints: companion object properties are static fields on the JVM
What happens when the Activity is destroyed but the static field still references it?

questions.title: Debugging Decision Tree
questions.description: Describe the systematic approach for debugging an Android app that is experiencing janky scrolling. What tools would you use in what order, and what would you look for in each?
questions.answer: For janky scrolling: (1) Start with Perfetto/Systrace to see the frame timeline — look at Choreographer#doFrame slices exceeding 16.6ms. Identify which phase is slow: measure/layout/draw or a blocking binder call. (2) If frames show long measure/layout, use Layout Inspector to check view hierarchy depth or excessive Compose recompositions during scroll. (3) If the Memory Profiler shows a sawtooth pattern during scrolling, you have GC pressure — allocating objects in onBindViewHolder() or composable lambdas. (4) Use CPU Profiler with sample-based recording to find hot methods during scroll. (5) Check for StrictMode violations — disk reads triggered by scrolling (e.g., loading preferences per item).
questions.explanation: Systematic debugging avoids guessing. Perfetto gives the big picture (which frames are slow and why). Layout Inspector and Memory Profiler zoom into specific causes. The key is to let the data guide you rather than assuming the cause — a janky scroll could be caused by main thread blocking, excessive recomposition, GC pressure, or even a slow content provider query.
hints: Start broad (Perfetto — whole system view) then narrow down
Frame budget is 16.6ms at 60fps — any frame exceeding this is janky
Consider multiple possible causes: CPU, memory, IPC, rendering

questions.title: Diagnosing a Streaming Jank Trace
questions.description: You are debugging dropped frames during AI response streaming. You capture a Perfetto trace and see this on the main thread for a single frame. What is the root cause and what is the correct fix?
questions.code:
Main Thread (one frame during streaming):

[Choreographer#doFrame ────── 54ms ──────]
  [traversal ────── 48ms ──────]
    [measure ────── 45ms ──────]
      [Text.measure ── 44ms ──]

RenderThread: [syncAndDraw 2ms]  ← GPU is fine
No binder transactions in this frame.
options: The RenderThread is too slow — GPU overdraw is the problem
A binder transaction is blocking the frame — move IPC off main thread

Text.measure is taking 44ms — the Text composable is remeasuring the entire AI response string every frame. Fix: chunk the response into smaller Text elements in a LazyColumn so only visible text is measured
The draw phase is too slow — simplify the visual hierarchy and reduce overdraw
questions.explanation: The trace clearly shows: measure dominates the frame at 45ms, and within measure, Text.measure takes 44ms. This happens when a single Text composable contains a very long string (the full AI response) — Compose must measure the entire text to determine layout, even if most of it is off-screen. The RenderThread (2ms) and draw phase are fine, so it's not a GPU problem. The fix: break the response into paragraphs or lines and display them in a LazyColumn so only visible text elements are measured. Alternative: use BasicText with a fixed width constraint to reduce measurement complexity.
hints: Look at which phase of doFrame takes the most time — it's not draw or GPU
What happens when you ask Compose to measure a 10,000-character string every frame?

questions.title: ANR Debugging Methodology
questions.description:
You receive an ANR report from production. The main thread stack trace shows:
main thread:
  at android.os.BinderProxy.transactNative(Native Method)
  at android.os.BinderProxy.transact(BinderProxy.java:584)
  at android.content.ContentProviderProxy.query(ContentProviderNative.java:472)
  at android.content.ContentResolver.query(ContentResolver.java:944)
  at com.example.app.ContactsRepository.searchContacts(ContactsRepository.kt:47)
  at com.example.app.SearchViewModel.onQueryChanged(SearchViewModel.kt:23)
  at com.example.app.SearchScreen$SearchBar.invoke(SearchScreen.kt:89)

What caused the ANR and how should you fix it?
options: The ContentProvider crashed — catch the exception and show an error message
BinderProxy.transact is a framework bug — file a bug against AOSP

A synchronous ContentResolver.query() call is running on the main thread via a Compose event handler, blocking for 5+ seconds while the contacts provider processes the query. Fix: move the query to withContext(Dispatchers.IO) in the ViewModel
The SearchBar composable is recomposing too many times — fix parameter stability
questions.explanation: The stack trace tells the whole story, read from bottom to top: The user types in a SearchBar (Compose) → onQueryChanged in ViewModel → searchContacts in Repository → ContentResolver.query() → synchronous binder transaction to the contacts ContentProvider. This IPC call blocked the main thread for 5+ seconds, triggering the ANR. The fix: suspend fun searchContacts() = withContext(Dispatchers.IO) { contentResolver.query(...) }. StrictMode with detectNetwork() and detectDiskReads() would have caught this in development — ContentProvider queries go through binder (IPC) and often involve disk reads on the provider side.
hints: Read the stack trace from bottom (user action) to top (where it's stuck)
BinderProxy.transact is synchronous IPC — the main thread waits until the other process responds
What tool would have caught this during development?
